findings.description: HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS) header is missing. Users may be vulnerable to SSL stripping attacks.
findings.solution: Add Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload header.
findings.recommendation: Add Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload header.

findings.description: The site is served over HTTP without SSL/TLS encryption. All data transmitted between the browser and server is in plaintext and can be intercepted.
findings.solution: Configure your server to use HTTPS. Obtain a free TLS certificate from Let's Encrypt (certbot) and redirect all HTTP traffic to HTTPS.
findings.recommendation: Configure your server to use HTTPS. Obtain a free TLS certificate from Let's Encrypt (certbot) and redirect all HTTP traffic to HTTPS.

findings.description: HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS) header is not set. Browsers will not automatically enforce HTTPS connections.
findings.solution: Add Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload to all HTTPS responses.
findings.recommendation: Add Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload to all HTTPS responses.

findings.description: The response does not protect against 'ClickJacking' attacks. It should include either Content-Security-Policy with 'frame-ancestors' directive or X-Frame-Options.
findings.solution:
Modern Web browsers support the Content-Security-Policy and X-Frame-Options HTTP headers. Ensure one of them is set on all web pages returned by your site/app.
If you expect the page to be framed only by pages on your server (e.g. it's part of a FRAMESET) then you'll want to use SAMEORIGIN, otherwise if you never expect the page to be framed, you should use DENY. Alternatively consider implementing Content Security Policy's "frame-ancestors" directive.

findings.vuln_type: Content Security Policy Header Not Set
findings.description: Content Security Policy (CSP) is an added layer of security that helps to detect and mitigate certain types of attacks, including Cross Site Scripting (XSS) and data injection attacks. These attacks are used for everything from data theft to site defacement or distribution of malware. CSP provides a set of standard HTTP headers that allow website owners to declare approved sources of content that browsers should be allowed to load on that page — covered types are JavaScript, CSS, HTML frames, fonts, images and embeddable objects such as Java applets, ActiveX, audio and video files.
findings.solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to set the Content-Security-Policy header.

findings.vuln_type: Content Security Policy Header Not Set
findings.description: Content Security Policy (CSP) is an added layer of security that helps to detect and mitigate certain types of attacks, including Cross Site Scripting (XSS) and data injection attacks. These attacks are used for everything from data theft to site defacement or distribution of malware. CSP provides a set of standard HTTP headers that allow website owners to declare approved sources of content that browsers should be allowed to load on that page — covered types are JavaScript, CSS, HTML frames, fonts, images and embeddable objects such as Java applets, ActiveX, audio and video files.
findings.solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to set the Content-Security-Policy header.

findings.description: It is possible to view the directory listing. Directory listing may reveal hidden scripts, include files, backup source files, etc. which can be accessed to read sensitive information.
findings.solution: Disable directory browsing. If this is required, make sure the listed files does not induce risks.

findings.description: It is possible to view the directory listing. Directory listing may reveal hidden scripts, include files, backup source files, etc. which can be accessed to read sensitive information.
findings.solution: Disable directory browsing. If this is required, make sure the listed files does not induce risks.

findings.description: It is possible to view the directory listing. Directory listing may reveal hidden scripts, include files, backup source files, etc. which can be accessed to read sensitive information.
findings.solution: Disable directory browsing. If this is required, make sure the listed files does not induce risks.

findings.description: The response does not protect against 'ClickJacking' attacks. It should include either Content-Security-Policy with 'frame-ancestors' directive or X-Frame-Options.
findings.solution:
Modern Web browsers support the Content-Security-Policy and X-Frame-Options HTTP headers. Ensure one of them is set on all web pages returned by your site/app.
If you expect the page to be framed only by pages on your server (e.g. it's part of a FRAMESET) then you'll want to use SAMEORIGIN, otherwise if you never expect the page to be framed, you should use DENY. Alternatively consider implementing Content Security Policy's "frame-ancestors" directive.

findings.description: This page contains an error/warning message that may disclose sensitive information like the location of the file that produced the unhandled exception. This information can be used to launch further attacks against the web application. The alert could be a false positive if the error message is found inside a documentation page.
findings.solution: Review the source code of this page. Implement custom error pages. Consider implementing a mechanism to provide a unique error reference/identifier to the client (browser) while logging the details on the server side and not exposing them to the user.

findings.description: It is possible to view a listing of the directory contents. Directory listings may reveal hidden scripts, include files, backup source files, etc., which can be accessed to reveal sensitive information.
findings.solution: Configure the web server to disable directory browsing.

findings.vuln_type: Content Security Policy Header Not Set
findings.description: Content Security Policy (CSP) is an added layer of security that helps to detect and mitigate certain types of attacks, including Cross Site Scripting (XSS) and data injection attacks. These attacks are used for everything from data theft to site defacement or distribution of malware. CSP provides a set of standard HTTP headers that allow website owners to declare approved sources of content that browsers should be allowed to load on that page — covered types are JavaScript, CSS, HTML frames, fonts, images and embeddable objects such as Java applets, ActiveX, audio and video files.
findings.solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to set the Content-Security-Policy header.

findings.description: Content Security Policy (CSP) header is not set. This allows attackers to inject malicious scripts via XSS attacks.
findings.solution: Add Content-Security-Policy header. Example: Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'; object-src 'none'
findings.recommendation: Add Content-Security-Policy header. Example: Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'; object-src 'none'

findings.description: X-Frame-Options header is missing. The site may be vulnerable to clickjacking attacks where an attacker embeds the page in an iframe.
findings.solution: Add X-Frame-Options: DENY or SAMEORIGIN header. For Nginx: add_header X-Frame-Options DENY; For Apache: Header always set X-Frame-Options DENY
findings.recommendation: Add X-Frame-Options: DENY or SAMEORIGIN header. For Nginx: add_header X-Frame-Options DENY; For Apache: Header always set X-Frame-Options DENY

findings.description: A cookie has been set without the HttpOnly flag, which means that the cookie can be accessed by JavaScript. If a malicious script can be run on this page then the cookie will be accessible and can be transmitted to another site. If this is a session cookie then session hijacking may be possible.
findings.solution: Ensure that the HttpOnly flag is set for all cookies.

findings.description: A cookie has been set without the SameSite attribute, which means that the cookie can be sent as a result of a 'cross-site' request. The SameSite attribute is an effective counter measure to cross-site request forgery, cross-site script inclusion, and timing attacks.
findings.solution: Ensure that the SameSite attribute is set to either 'lax' or ideally 'strict' for all cookies.

findings.description: The web/application server is leaking version information via the "Server" HTTP response header. Access to such information may facilitate attackers identifying other vulnerabilities your web/application server is subject to.
findings.solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to suppress the "Server" header or provide generic details.

findings.description: The web/application server is leaking version information via the "Server" HTTP response header. Access to such information may facilitate attackers identifying other vulnerabilities your web/application server is subject to.
findings.solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to suppress the "Server" header or provide generic details.

findings.description: The Anti-MIME-Sniffing header X-Content-Type-Options was not set to 'nosniff'. This allows older versions of Internet Explorer and Chrome to perform MIME-sniffing on the response body, potentially causing the response body to be interpreted and displayed as a content type other than the declared content type. Current (early 2014) and legacy versions of Firefox will use the declared content type (if one is set), rather than performing MIME-sniffing.
findings.solution:
Ensure that the application/web server sets the Content-Type header appropriately, and that it sets the X-Content-Type-Options header to 'nosniff' for all web pages.
If possible, ensure that the end user uses a standards-compliant and modern web browser that does not perform MIME-sniffing at all, or that can be directed by the web application/web server to not perform MIME-sniffing.

findings.description: The server returned a version banner string in the response content. Such information leaks may allow attackers to further target specific issues impacting the product and version in use.
findings.solution:
Configure the server to prevent such information leaks. For example:
Under Tomcat this is done via the "server" directive and implementation of custom error pages.
Under Apache this is done via the "ServerSignature" and "ServerTokens" directives.

findings.description: The web/application server is leaking version information via the "Server" HTTP response header. Access to such information may facilitate attackers identifying other vulnerabilities your web/application server is subject to.
findings.solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to suppress the "Server" header or provide generic details.

findings.description: The Anti-MIME-Sniffing header X-Content-Type-Options was not set to 'nosniff'. This allows older versions of Internet Explorer and Chrome to perform MIME-sniffing on the response body, potentially causing the response body to be interpreted and displayed as a content type other than the declared content type. Current (early 2014) and legacy versions of Firefox will use the declared content type (if one is set), rather than performing MIME-sniffing.
findings.solution:
Ensure that the application/web server sets the Content-Type header appropriately, and that it sets the X-Content-Type-Options header to 'nosniff' for all web pages.
If possible, ensure that the end user uses a standards-compliant and modern web browser that does not perform MIME-sniffing at all, or that can be directed by the web application/web server to not perform MIME-sniffing.

findings.description: The Anti-MIME-Sniffing header X-Content-Type-Options was not set to 'nosniff'. findings.solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to suppress the "Server" header or provide generic details.

findings.description: The Anti-MIME-Sniffing header X-Content-Type-Options was not set to 'nosniff'. This allows older versions of Internet Explorer and Chrome to perform MIME-sniffing on the response body, potentially causing the response body to be interpreted and displayed as a content type other than the declared content type. Current (early 2014) and legacy versions of Firefox will use the declared content type (if one is set), rather than performing MIME-sniffing.
findings.solution:
Ensure that the application/web server sets the Content-Type header appropriately, and that it sets the X-Content-Type-Options header to 'nosniff' for all web pages.
If possible, ensure that the end user uses a standards-compliant and modern web browser that does not perform MIME-sniffing at all, or that can be directed by the web application/web server to not perform MIME-sniffing.

findings.description: This page contains an error/warning message that may disclose sensitive information like the location of the file that produced the unhandled exception. This information can be used to launch further attacks against the web application. The alert could be a false positive if the error message is found inside a documentation page.
findings.solution: Review the source code of this page. Implement custom error pages. Consider implementing a mechanism to provide a unique error reference/identifier to the client (browser) while logging the details on the server side and not exposing them to the user.

If possible, ensure that the end user uses a standards-compliant and modern web browser that does not perform MIME-sniffing at all, or that can be directed by the web application/web server to not perform MIME-sniffing.

findings.description: The server returned a version banner string in the response content. Such information leaks may allow attackers to further target specific issues impacting the product and version in use.
findings.solution:
Configure the server to prevent such information leaks. For example:
Under Tomcat this is done via the "server" directive and implementation of custom error pages.
Under Apache this is done via the "ServerSignature" and "ServerTokens" directives.

findings.description: The web/application server is leaking version information via the "Server" HTTP response header. Access to such information may facilitate attackers identifying other vulnerabilities your web/application server is subject to.
findings.solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to suppress the "Server" header or provide generic details.

findings.description: X-Content-Type-Options header is not set. Browsers may perform MIME-type sniffing, potentially executing malicious content.
findings.solution: Add X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff header to all responses.
findings.recommendation: Add X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff header to all responses.

findings.description: Referrer-Policy header is not set. Sensitive URL parameters may leak to third parties via the Referer header.
findings.solution: Add Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin or no-referrer header.
findings.recommendation: Add Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin or no-referrer header.

findings.description: Permissions-Policy header is missing. Browser features like camera, microphone, and geolocation are not restricted.
findings.solution: Add Permissions-Policy header. Example: Permissions-Policy: camera=(), microphone=(), geolocation=()
findings.recommendation: Add Permissions-Policy header. Example: Permissions-Policy: camera=(), microphone=(), geolocation=()

findings.description: X-XSS-Protection header is not set. While deprecated in modern browsers, older browsers lack XSS filter activation.
findings.solution: Add X-XSS-Protection: 1; mode=block header for legacy browser support.
findings.recommendation: Add X-XSS-Protection: 1; mode=block header for legacy browser support.

findings.description: Server header reveals web server software and version, aiding attackers in targeting known vulnerabilities.
findings.solution: Configure your web server to suppress or genericise the Server header. For Nginx: server_tokens off; For Apache: ServerTokens Prod
findings.recommendation: Configure your web server to suppress or genericise the Server header. For Nginx: server_tokens off; For Apache: ServerTokens Prod
